task.prompt: Should we choose space or tab when writing Swift?
task.title: Swift indentation policy: spaces or tabs?

finalClaims.title: Swift community standard is spaces
finalClaims.statement: The Swift open-source community, Apple's own standard library, and major style guides (Swift.org, Google Swift Style Guide) all mandate space-based indentation, making spaces the de facto standard for Swift code.

finalClaims.title: Xcode defaults to spaces
finalClaims.statement: Xcode, the primary IDE for Swift development, defaults to 4-space indentation and auto-indents with spaces, meaning using tabs requires actively overriding the tooling default. This 4-space default aligns with the Swift standard library and Apple's official swift-format tool.

finalClaims.title: Spaces render consistently across viewers
finalClaims.statement: Spaces produce identical visual output in all contexts (GitHub, terminals, editors), while tabs render at different widths depending on editor configuration, causing inconsistency in code review and documentation.

finalClaims.title: Tabs enable per-developer display customization
finalClaims.statement: Tabs can improve per-developer readability via configurable width, but in Swift teams enforcing SwiftFormat/SwiftLint, this benefit is mostly local and should not drive shared source style.

finalClaims.title: Tabs are semantically intended for indentation
finalClaims.statement: The historical convention that tabs represent indentation is valid context, but it has low decision weight in Swift compared with ecosystem and tooling consistency.

finalClaims.title: SwiftLint enforces space indentation by default
finalClaims.statement: SwiftLint, the most widely adopted Swift linter, and SwiftFormat, Apple's official formatter, both enforce space-based indentation by default. Teams using either tool in CI automatically align with space convention without additional configuration. Switching to tabs requires overriding both tools' defaults and creates ongoing formatter churn.

finalClaims.title: Ecosystem lock-in to spaces creates practical costs for tabs
finalClaims.statement: Even if tabs were theoretically superior, the Swift ecosystem's strong standardization on spaces means using tabs introduces real friction: linter conflicts, inconsistent code review rendering, and collaboration friction with other Swift developers.

finalClaims.title: 2 spaces is the specific Swift convention
finalClaims.statement: There is no single universal Swift indentation width across all guides; in practice, 4 spaces is the dominant default (especially in Xcode-based workflows), while some guides use 2. Teams should pick one width and enforce it consistently.

finalClaims.title: Ecosystem Convention Favors Spaces
finalClaims.statement: Most Swift style guides, templates, and team tooling assume space-based indentation, so choosing spaces minimizes surprise and onboarding friction.

finalClaims.title: Spaces Improve Diff Predictability
finalClaims.statement: Space-indented code tends to produce more predictable visual alignment across editors and code review UIs, reducing review misunderstandings and noisy churn.

finalClaims.title: Tabs Improve Personal Readability
finalClaims.statement: Tabs let each developer choose preferred indentation width, which can improve accessibility and reduce eye strain for some contributors.

finalClaims.title: Formatter-First Teams Benefit from Spaces
finalClaims.statement: In Swift projects using automatic formatting, spaces integrate more consistently with formatter output and reduce editor-specific interpretation differences.

finalClaims.title: Tabs Create Cross-Tool Rendering Risk
finalClaims.statement: When tools render tab width differently, alignment-sensitive Swift code and review context can appear inconsistent, increasing coordination overhead.

finalClaims.title: Adopt a Hybrid Enforcement Policy
finalClaims.statement: Enforcement should validate leading indentation style and avoid blanket tab bans in non-indentation contexts, as part of the main hybrid enforcement policy.

finalClaims.title: Apple's swift-format officially endorses spaces
finalClaims.statement: Treat swift-format’s default space indentation as evidence under the main tooling-default claim rather than as a separate standalone claim.

finalClaims.title: Enforcement should target indentation, not every tab byte
finalClaims.statement: Whitespace policy should explicitly validate leading indentation style in Swift files, while avoiding blanket tab bans that can create false positives in non-indentation content such as string literals or generated text.

votes.reason: The strongest single claim in the catalog. Community standard, official style guides, and Apple's own source code all point to spaces. Uncontested across all rounds.
Strong and accurate primary claim for choosing spaces.
Tooling defaults carry high practical weight. Xcode's 4-space default and swift-format alignment mean choosing tabs requires active overrides in the most common Swift development environment.
Useful directionally, but factual overreach in tool-alignment details; needs revision.
Rendering consistency is a verifiable, objective property. This claim directly explains why tabs create real friction in code review and documentation contexts.
High practical value for review and collaboration consistency.
The strongest honest argument for tabs. Accept it to keep the catalog intellectually credible. The formatter-enforcement caveat is correctly integrated.
Valid secondary consideration that does not overturn the main decision.
Historically valid, appropriately downweighted. Including it demonstrates the debate examined tab arguments in good faith before concluding for spaces.
Valid contextual point with appropriately low decision weight.
Specific, named-tool evidence with concrete consequence (formatter churn). Among the most practically useful claims for a team making this decision.
Core idea is good but wording is factually imprecise and overly absolute; should be revised.
Important nuance: the spaces-vs-tabs verdict does not resolve the 2-vs-4 width question. This claim correctly separates the two decisions and prevents overclaiming.
Correctly frames width as a separate team-level consistency choice.
Turns the debate conclusion into an operational policy. The enforcement-scoping detail (indentation only, not blanket tab ban) is practically important for avoiding linter false positives.
Actionable enforcement policy that improves correctness without noisy false positives.

representative.speech: Final vote: choose spaces for Swift source indentation. Ecosystem convention, editor defaults, formatter/linter workflows, and cross-view consistency all favor spaces for shared codebases. Tabs still offer per-developer display flexibility, but that advantage is mostly local and should not drive repository-wide style in Swift teams. Enforcement should focus on leading indentation style in source code rather than banning every tab byte in all contexts. Indentation width is a separate policy choice; pick one width and enforce it consistently.

report.finalSummary: Consensus favors spaces for Swift indentation: they align with ecosystem norms and tooling, while tab benefits are mostly local and should not drive shared repository style.
report.representativeSpeech: After evaluating standards, tooling behavior, and collaboration impact, the recommendation is to use spaces for leading indentation in Swift source. This aligns with prevailing Swift practices, improves cross-view consistency in reviews, and minimizes formatter/linter friction in team and CI workflows. Tabs remain useful for personal display preferences, but that advantage is local and is better handled through editor settings than repository policy. Choose one indentation width as a separate team decision, enforce it consistently, and scope checks to indentation rather than every tab character.

outputs.fullResponse:
The question of spaces vs. tabs in Swift is largely settled by the Swift community and Apple's own tooling, but it's worth examining the underlying arguments carefully.

Swift's official style guide (Swift.org) and the most widely-used community style guide (Google's Swift Style Guide) both mandate 2-space indentation. Xcode, the primary IDE for Swift development, defaults to spaces. These facts alone create a strong practical argument for spaces.

**Arguments for Spaces:**
1. Community standard: The overwhelming consensus in the Swift ecosystem is spaces (typically 2). Swift Evolution proposals, Apple's own Swift standard library source code, and virtually every major open-source Swift project use spaces.
2. Xcode default: Xcode auto-indents with spaces. Fighting your IDE is friction with no benefit.
3. Consistent rendering: Spaces render identically everywhere—GitHub, terminals, editors—regardless of tab-width settings. Tabs render differently depending on the viewer's configuration (2, 4, or 8 spaces), causing visual inconsistency in code review and documentation.
4. Copy-paste reliability: Code copied into playgrounds, REPL, or web forms maintains its visual structure with spaces.

**Arguments for Tabs:**
1. Semantic intent: Tabs are designed for indentation; spaces are for alignment. Using tabs for indentation preserves the semantic distinction.
2. Accessibility: Developers who need wider indentation for readability can configure tab width in their editor without touching the source file.
3. Smaller diffs: Tab-indented code produces smaller diffs when indentation levels change.
4. Editor neutrality: Tabs technically give each developer control over display width.

**Analysis:**
The accessibility argument for tabs is genuinely compelling in theory. However, in practice, Swift's ecosystem has standardized so thoroughly on spaces that using tabs would cause friction with linters (SwiftLint defaults to spaces), code review tools, and collaboration with other Swift developers. The practical costs of deviating from the ecosystem standard far outweigh the theoretical benefits of tabs.

The verdict is clear: use spaces (2 spaces) when writing Swift. This is not a matter of strong personal preference—it's a matter of aligning with the ecosystem you are working in.

outputs.summary: Swift's ecosystem has standardized on 2-space indentation. Official guides, Xcode defaults, and community practice all favor spaces. Tabs offer theoretical benefits (accessibility, semantic clarity) but create real friction in the Swift toolchain and collaboration workflows.
outputs.taskTitle: Spaces vs. Tabs: The Indentation Style Choice for Swift

outputs.fullResponse: My initial position is to standardize on spaces for Swift indentation in shared codebases. Swift teams optimize for diff stability, formatter compatibility, and ecosystem convention, and spaces win on all three in practice. Tabs offer stronger per-developer visual flexibility, which is a legitimate accessibility and preference advantage, but that benefit is smaller when projects already rely on automatic formatting and consistent rendered width in code review tools. A pragmatic policy is: spaces for leading indentation, allow tabs only where semantically required (for example, inside string literals), and enforce via SwiftFormat/SwiftLint plus editorconfig. The debate should focus on measurable costs: merge noise, formatter behavior, onboarding friction, and accessibility impact.

extractedClaims.statement: Most Swift style guides, templates, and team tooling assume space-based indentation, so choosing spaces minimizes surprise and onboarding friction.

extractedClaims.title: Spaces Improve Diff Predictability
extractedClaims.statement: Space-indented code tends to produce more predictable visual alignment across editors and code review UIs, reducing review misunderstandings and noisy churn.

extractedClaims.title: Tabs Improve Personal Readability
extractedClaims.statement: Tabs let each developer choose preferred indentation width, which can improve accessibility and reduce eye strain for some contributors.

extractedClaims.title: Formatter-First Teams Benefit from Spaces
extractedClaims.statement: In Swift projects using automatic formatting, spaces integrate more consistently with formatter output and reduce editor-specific interpretation differences.

extractedClaims.title: Tabs Create Cross-Tool Rendering Risk
extractedClaims.statement: When tools render tab width differently, alignment-sensitive Swift code and review context can appear inconsistent, increasing coordination overhead.

extractedClaims.title: Adopt a Hybrid Enforcement Policy
extractedClaims.statement: Set team policy to spaces for leading indentation, permit tabs only in content where whitespace is semantic, and enforce with lint/format checks in CI.

outputs.summary: Use spaces as the default indentation for Swift team code, with tooling enforcement and narrow exceptions, while acknowledging tabs as a readability/accessibility tradeoff.
outputs.taskTitle: Swift indentation policy: spaces or tabs?

outputs.fullResponse:
Reviewing the full claim catalog after seeing codex-agent's round 0 response, several observations stand out.

First, there are clear duplicates across the two agents' submissions that should be merged. codex-agent:0:0 ('Ecosystem Convention Favors Spaces') covers the same ground as claude-agent:0:0 ('Swift community standard is spaces') — both make the community standardization argument. The earlier claim should survive. Similarly, codex-agent:0:1 ('Spaces Improve Diff Predictability') largely restates claude-agent:0:2 ('Spaces render consistently across viewers'), since both argue spaces give uniform visual output across tools. codex-agent:0:2 ('Tabs Improve Personal Readability') duplicates claude-agent:0:3 ('Tabs enable per-developer display customization'). And codex-agent:0:4 ('Tabs Create Cross-Tool Rendering Risk') is substantially the same claim as claude-agent:0:6 ('Ecosystem lock-in to spaces creates practical costs for tabs'), both focusing on tab rendering inconsistency across tools.

Second, claude-agent:0:3 and claude-agent:0:4 deserve revision in light of the codex-agent's point about formatter-first workflows. When teams enforce SwiftFormat or SwiftLint in CI, the accessibility benefit of tabs (per-developer width configuration) is largely nullified — the formatter normalizes the output regardless. The claim about tabs enabling personal customization should acknowledge this constraint. Similarly, claude-agent:0:4 (the semantic-intent argument for tabs) is a philosophical observation with almost no practical weight in Swift development; it should be downgraded to a footnote-level consideration.

Third, codex-agent:0:3 ('Formatter-First Teams Benefit from Spaces') and codex-agent:0:5 ('Adopt a Hybrid Enforcement Policy') are genuinely additive. The formatter-first framing is a distinct practical angle — it explains *why* spaces win even for teams sympathetic to tabs. The hybrid enforcement policy claim is also worth keeping as a concrete action item, though it should be refined: in practice, tabs inside Swift string literals are already whitespace-insignificant content, so 'permit tabs only in content where whitespace is semantic' is a bit of a non-issue — the real policy is simply 'use spaces everywhere for indentation, enforce via .editorconfig + SwiftLint + SwiftFormat in CI.'

Overall, both agents converge on the same conclusion. The catalog is richer for having both perspectives, but needs deduplication and a few refinements to be maximally useful.

judgements.rationale: Well-established fact. Swift.org, Google Swift Style Guide, and Apple's own standard library source all use spaces. This is the strongest single argument for spaces.
Factually accurate. Xcode's default indentation is 4 spaces (not 2, notably), and auto-indent uses spaces. Working against IDE defaults is unnecessary friction.
Correct. Spaces have a fixed width by definition; tabs render at editor-configured widths (typically 2, 4, or 8). This directly causes visual inconsistency in GitHub PR reviews and documentation.

The accessibility/customization argument for tabs is legitimate in isolation, but significantly weakened in formatter-enforced workflows. If SwiftFormat or SwiftLint rewrites files on save or in CI, per-developer tab width preferences are overridden anyway.
judgements.revisedStatement: Tabs allow each developer to configure their preferred indentation width in their editor without modifying the source file, which can benefit accessibility and personal comfort — however, this advantage is largely nullified in teams that enforce automatic formatters (SwiftFormat, SwiftLint) in CI, since the formatter normalizes indentation regardless of the developer's local preference.

judgements.rationale: The semantic-intent argument is a philosophical observation that has essentially no practical weight in Swift development. The Swift ecosystem never adopted tabs despite this theoretical correctness. Worth noting as historical context but should not be treated as a strong argument for tabs in practice.
judgements.revisedStatement: While the tab character was originally designed for indentation (versus spaces for alignment within lines), this semantic distinction has little practical relevance in modern Swift development, where ecosystem tooling has universally standardized on spaces regardless of original intent.

judgements.rationale: Accurate. SwiftLint's indentation rule defaults to 4 spaces and flags tab indentation. Deviating from this requires explicit configuration changes that will also conflict with most shared SwiftLint configs.
Strong risk claim. The practical friction of using tabs in a Swift codebase (linter conflicts, rendering inconsistencies in PR review, onboarding confusion) outweighs any theoretical tab benefits.

Valid tradeoff point. The Swift convention is specifically 4 spaces in Xcode's default, though 2 spaces appears in some style guides. Either way, a specific space count must be chosen, which is an argument for explicit tooling enforcement.
This claim covers the same ground as claude-agent:0:0 (community/ecosystem standardization on spaces). The earlier claim should be the canonical one.

The 'diff predictability' framing is largely the same argument as claude-agent:0:2 (consistent rendering across viewers). Tabs rendering at different widths is exactly what causes unpredictable diffs and review inconsistencies. The earlier claim should survive.
This is a restatement of claude-agent:0:3 (per-developer display customization). The earlier claim already covers accessibility and personal preference. Should be merged.

Genuinely distinct angle from codex-agent. The formatter-first framing explains specifically why spaces integrate more cleanly with automated tooling — SwiftFormat produces space-indented output by default, so mixing tabs in source creates churn on every formatter pass.
This claim covers the same risk as claude-agent:0:6 (ecosystem lock-in costs) and claude-agent:0:2 (rendering inconsistency). The rendering risk is already captured in the earlier claims.

The hybrid enforcement policy is a useful concrete recommendation, but the framing 'permit tabs only where semantically required (e.g., string literals)' is a near-nonissue — tabs in string literals are literal content, not indentation, and nobody debates those. The real actionable policy is simpler: enforce spaces for all indentation via .editorconfig + SwiftLint + SwiftFormat in CI.
judgements.revisedStatement: Establish a team policy of space-based indentation for all Swift source code and enforce it automatically via .editorconfig, SwiftLint, and SwiftFormat integrated into CI. This eliminates individual style drift and makes the spaces-vs-tabs debate moot in day-to-day development.

outputs.summary: Both agents agree: spaces win for Swift indentation due to ecosystem standardization, tooling defaults, and consistent rendering. Several codex-agent claims duplicate earlier claude-agent claims and should be merged. The formatter-first framing and enforcement policy claims from codex-agent are genuinely additive. Two of the pro-tab claims need revision to acknowledge their reduced practical weight in formatter-enforced workflows.

outputs.fullResponse: The strongest case for Swift is still spaces, specifically 2-space indentation, because interoperability costs dominate theoretical purity. Claims about ecosystem convention, Xcode defaults, and lint/formatter defaults reinforce each other and should be treated as one practical constraint set: choosing tabs creates avoidable team friction. Several claims are duplicates across participants and should be merged to reduce noise. The pro-tab arguments (semantic intent and personal width control) are valid but should be reframed as secondary tradeoffs rather than decision-driving factors in typical Swift teams. Net position: default to spaces (2), enforce in tooling, and handle readability needs through editor settings rather than repository indentation divergence.

judgements.rationale: This is the central practical claim: Swift collaboration norms and published style guides make spaces the least-friction default.
Duplicate of the earlier ecosystem-standard claim.
Tool defaults matter in day-to-day velocity; going against Xcode defaults adds recurring local friction.
Cross-editor/render consistency is a concrete collaboration benefit, especially in PR review and mixed tooling environments.
Substantively overlaps with the rendering-consistency claim and should be consolidated.
This is the risk-framed restatement of the same cross-tool tab-width inconsistency point.
Per-developer width control is a real benefit of tabs, but it is a secondary concern relative to ecosystem compatibility.
Duplicate of the accessibility/readability argument for tabs.
The semantic-history argument is technically true but weak as a modern decision criterion in Swift teams.
judgements.revisedStatement: Tabs being historically intended for indentation is a valid convention argument, but in Swift it is outweighed by ecosystem and tooling consistency requirements.

judgements.rationale: Default linter expectations are a practical enforcement reality and support choosing spaces.
This tooling point largely overlaps with the lint/formatter-default argument and should be unified.
This is the best synthesis claim: even if tabs have local benefits, ecosystem lock-in makes divergence costly.
The width convention detail is important for actionable guidance: in Swift, spaces usually implies 2 spaces.
A policy-level recommendation is useful and operationally sound: define indentation rules and enforce via CI tooling.

outputs.summary: Use 2-space indentation in Swift; merge duplicate claims around ecosystem/tooling consistency, keep tab benefits as secondary tradeoffs.

outputs.fullResponse:
Entering round 2, the claim catalog is now well-consolidated from the merges in round 1. The active claims cover the main arguments effectively. This round I will focus on accuracy corrections, strengthening the remaining active claims, and adding one genuinely new point.

The most important factual issue to address is claude-agent:0:7 ('2 spaces is the specific Swift convention'). This claim is imprecise and partially inaccurate. Xcode's built-in indentation default is 4 spaces, not 2. The Swift standard library source code uses 4 spaces. Apple's official swift-format tool defaults to 4 spaces. The Ray Wenderlich (now Kodeco) Swift style guide specifies 4 spaces. Google's Swift Style Guide is the notable exception recommending 2 spaces. The claim as written overstates the 2-space convention and could mislead. It should be revised to reflect that the practical ecosystem default is actually 4 spaces, with Google's guide as a dissenting voice.

The second factual refinement: claude-agent:0:1 states Xcode 'defaults to space indentation' — this is correct directionally but vague. Xcode's specific default is 4 spaces, which directly supports the broader ecosystem argument and resolves the ambiguity created by claim 7.

For claude-agent:0:5, the claim now reflects SwiftLint's default behavior, but it omits Apple's own official swift-format tool, which is increasingly important. Apple released swift-format as the canonical formatter for the Swift open-source project itself. swift-format uses spaces (specifically 2-space indentation in its default configuration for Swift code style, though it can be configured). Mentioning swift-format strengthens this claim substantially because it represents Apple's official tooling position.

codex-agent:0:5 (the enforcement policy claim) is solid and actionable. The codex-agent's position in round 1 — treat readability preferences through editor settings rather than repository indentation — aligns well with this. The claim should be agreed.

All four remaining pro-spaces claims (0:0, 0:1, 0:2, 0:5) form a mutually reinforcing argument: community standard + tooling default + rendering consistency + linter enforcement = overwhelming practical case for spaces. The two pro-tab claims (0:3, 0:4) are correctly positioned as secondary considerations that do not override this practical consensus.

One new claim worth adding: Apple's swift-format being an official tool that defaults to spaces is distinct enough from the SwiftLint claim to stand on its own, as it represents Apple's direct tooling endorsement rather than a third-party linter default.

extractedClaims.statement: Apple's swift-format, released as the official formatter for the Swift open-source project and recommended for Swift Package Manager projects, uses space-based indentation by default. This represents Apple's direct tooling endorsement of spaces, not merely community convention or third-party linter defaults.

judgements.rationale: Solid, well-supported claim. Now represents both agents' positions on community standardization. The statement accurately captures Swift.org, Apple standard library, and Google Swift Style Guide consensus on spaces.

The claim correctly states Xcode defaults to spaces but omits the specific default width (4 spaces), which is directly relevant to the correction needed in claim claude-agent:0:7. Making the 4-space default explicit here strengthens the overall argument.
judgements.revisedStatement: Xcode, the primary IDE for Swift development, defaults to 4-space indentation and auto-indents with spaces, meaning using tabs requires actively overriding the tooling default. This 4-space default aligns with the Swift standard library and Apple's official swift-format tool.

judgements.rationale: Strong consolidated claim that now absorbs the diff predictability and cross-tool rendering risk arguments. Accurately captures why tabs create visual inconsistency in code review contexts.

The revised statement correctly acknowledges the formatter-enforcement caveat that substantially weakens the tab customization argument in practice. Well-balanced as a con that is acknowledged but not decision-driving.

Appropriately downgraded from a strong argument to a historical footnote. The revised framing correctly positions the semantic intent argument as interesting trivia rather than a practical decision factor for Swift development.

The claim is accurate but should explicitly include Apple's official swift-format tool, which was released as the canonical formatter for the Swift open-source project. Including swift-format alongside SwiftLint significantly strengthens this claim because it represents Apple's direct tooling endorsement, not just third-party linter convention.
judgements.revisedStatement: SwiftLint, the most widely-used Swift linter, enforces space-based indentation by default. Apple's official swift-format tool also uses space indentation. Adopting tabs requires overriding both tools' defaults and deviates from the standard linting and formatting setup used across the Swift ecosystem.

judgements.rationale: The claim that 'Swift convention specifically favors 2-space indentation' is factually imprecise. Xcode defaults to 4 spaces, the Swift standard library uses 4 spaces, Apple's swift-format defaults to 4 spaces, and the Kodeco (formerly Ray Wenderlich) style guide specifies 4 spaces. Google's Swift Style Guide is the primary source recommending 2 spaces. The claim overstates 2-space prevalence and should be corrected.
judgements.revisedStatement: The Swift ecosystem does not have a single universal space-count convention: Xcode, the Swift standard library, Apple's swift-format, and the Kodeco style guide all use 4-space indentation, while Google's Swift Style Guide recommends 2 spaces. Teams should choose one width, enforce it via tooling, and recognize that the spaces-vs-tabs question is separate from the 2-vs-4 question.

judgements.rationale: The revised enforcement policy claim is accurate and actionable. Enforcing via .editorconfig + SwiftLint + SwiftFormat in CI effectively eliminates the spaces-vs-tabs debate in practice and makes individual developer style preferences a non-issue for repository consistency.

outputs.summary: Round 2 focuses on accuracy and strengthening. The key correction is claude-agent:0:7: Swift's practical default is 4 spaces (Xcode, standard library, swift-format, Kodeco guide), not 2 — only Google's guide uses 2. claude-agent:0:1 should explicitly state the 4-space default. claude-agent:0:5 should be expanded to include Apple's official swift-format. Both agents converge on spaces; no substantive disagreement remains. The enforcement policy claim stands as the actionable conclusion.

outputs.fullResponse: The debate has converged on a practical outcome: choose spaces for Swift code. The highest-value claims are ecosystem convention, cross-tool rendering consistency, and default behavior of core tooling (Xcode/SwiftLint/formatters). I agree with the current duplicate handling and only recommend incremental refinement. Specifically, pro-tab arguments should be retained but clearly bounded: configurable tab width can help individual comfort, yet formatter-enforced teams largely neutralize that benefit in shared code; and the historical 'tabs are for indentation' argument is valid but low-impact for modern Swift collaboration. I also recommend softening the '2 spaces' claim so it does not read as universal truth; consistency within a repository matters more than asserting one global Swift width. Final recommendation remains: spaces for indentation, automated enforcement in CI, and policy language scoped to indentation rather than every tab character.

judgements.rationale: The policy is strong but should be precise about enforcement scope to avoid overreach.
judgements.revisedStatement: Adopt spaces for leading indentation in Swift source, enforce via .editorconfig + SwiftFormat + SwiftLint in CI, and scope checks to indentation rather than all tab characters.

outputs.summary: Use spaces in Swift; keep tooling/consistency claims central, downgrade tab arguments to limited caveats, and revise the 2-space claim to 'common but not universal.'

outputs.fullResponse:
Round 3 is a consolidation round. Both agents have converged on the same conclusion — spaces for Swift indentation, enforced via tooling — and the claim catalog accurately reflects this. My focus here is on two remaining cleanup issues and final confidence assessments.

First, claude-agent:0:5's statement has degraded into an internal meta-note ('Formatter- and linter-driven Swift workflows favor spaces in practice; keep this as supporting evidence under tooling-default claims'). This reads like a note to future editors rather than a standalone claim. It should be restored as a properly worded statement that conveys the actual evidence: SwiftLint and SwiftFormat both enforce space indentation by default, and this makes formatter-enforced workflows naturally space-aligned.

Second, codex-agent:2:0 ('Enforcement should target indentation, not every tab byte') is a useful clarification, but its substance is already captured in the revised codex-agent:0:5 ('scope checks to indentation rather than all tab characters'). The two claims say the same thing at slightly different levels of detail. The earlier claim (codex-agent:0:5) should survive; codex-agent:2:0 should be merged into it.

All other active claims are in good shape. The pro-spaces claims (0:0, 0:1, 0:2, 0:5, 2:0) form a coherent, mutually reinforcing argument. The pro-tab claims (0:3, 0:4) are correctly framed as secondary considerations that do not override the practical consensus. The tradeoff claim (0:7) is now properly scoped to emphasize intra-team consistency over universal prescription. The enforcement policy claim (codex-agent:0:5) is the appropriate actionable conclusion.

No new claims are needed. The debate is well-summarized by the existing active catalog. The verdict is unambiguous: spaces, enforced by tooling, with the specific width left as a team decision.

judgements.rationale: The central claim of this debate. Well-evidenced by Swift.org, Apple's standard library, and Google's style guide. No challenges have emerged across three rounds. Confirmed as the primary decision-driving claim.
Factually accurate with the 4-space correction from round 2. Xcode's default, the standard library, and swift-format all use spaces. The claim now correctly specifies 4-space as the dominant default.

Strong consolidated claim. The rendering inconsistency argument is one of the most practical and verifiable reasons to prefer spaces. Tabs genuinely do render differently across GitHub (8 spaces), VS Code (default 4), and terminal environments.

Well-balanced. Acknowledges the legitimate accessibility argument for tabs while correctly bounding its impact in formatter-enforced workflows. Appropriate as a con that is noted but does not drive the decision.

Correctly downgraded to a low-weight historical observation. Retaining it gives the catalog intellectual honesty — the argument for tabs has genuine historical grounding — but the claim clearly flags that it does not override ecosystem and tooling factors.

The current statement has degraded into meta-commentary rather than a substantive claim. 'Keep this as supporting evidence under tooling-default claims' is an internal note, not a claim. Restoring it to a proper declarative statement that captures both SwiftLint and SwiftFormat.
judgements.revisedStatement: SwiftLint, the most widely adopted Swift linter, and SwiftFormat, Apple's official formatter, both enforce space-based indentation by default. Teams using either tool in CI automatically align with space convention without additional configuration. Switching to tabs requires overriding both tools' defaults and creates ongoing formatter churn.

judgements.rationale: The revised statement correctly frames the 2-vs-4-space question as a team convention choice rather than a universal Swift rule. This is the right level of nuance: spaces are the answer to the spaces-vs-tabs question; width is a separate, lower-stakes decision.
Solid, actionable enforcement policy claim that correctly scopes the check to leading indentation rather than all tab bytes. This is the appropriate concluding recommendation for the debate.

Strong claim introduced in round 2. Apple's direct tooling endorsement via swift-format is qualitatively different from third-party linter convention — it represents the language steward's own position on indentation. This is among the strongest single pieces of evidence for spaces.

The enforcement-scoping guidance in this claim ('validate leading indentation, avoid blanket tab bans for string literals/generated content') is substantively identical to the final clause of codex-agent:0:5 ('scope checks to indentation rather than all tab characters'). The earlier claim should survive.

outputs.summary: Round 3 is final cleanup. claude-agent:0:5's statement needs restoration to a proper claim form. codex-agent:2:0 duplicates the enforcement-scoping language already in codex-agent:0:5 and should be merged. All remaining active claims are confirmed. The debate converges cleanly: use spaces in Swift, enforce via SwiftLint/SwiftFormat/.editorconfig in CI, and treat specific space width as a configurable team convention rather than a universal rule.

outputs.fullResponse: The catalog now supports a stable conclusion: choose spaces for Swift source files. The core argument is coherent and practical: ecosystem convention, Xcode’s default behavior (4 spaces), consistent rendering in reviews/tools, and formatter/linter-driven enforcement all point the same way. I recommend two refinements to reduce redundancy and factual ambiguity: revise the "2 spaces" claim to reflect that 4 spaces is the dominant default in practice (while 2-space guides exist), and merge duplicate subclaims into earlier anchors (swift-format evidence into the Xcode/tooling-default claim; indentation-only enforcement into the hybrid policy claim). Tabs remain a legitimate personal viewing preference, but that belongs in editor settings, not shared repository indentation style.

judgements.rationale: As a top-level claim, this correctly captures the decisive factor: Swift collaboration works best when following prevailing space-based ecosystem expectations.
This is now precise and strong: Xcode defaulting to 4 spaces is concrete, observable, and highly relevant for day-to-day Swift development.
Cross-tool rendering consistency is a practical collaboration concern, and this claim accurately explains why spaces reduce review ambiguity.
Keeping lint/formatter behavior as supporting evidence is appropriate; it reinforces, rather than replaces, the primary tooling-default argument.

The consistency point is good, but the specific framing overstates 2-space prevalence. In Swift practice, 4 spaces is the dominant default via Xcode and many Apple-centric workflows, with notable 2-space exceptions.
judgements.revisedStatement: There is no single universal Swift indentation width across all guides; in practice, 4 spaces is the dominant default (especially in Xcode-based workflows), while some guides use 2. Teams should pick one width and enforce it consistently.

judgements.rationale: This is valuable evidence but overlaps with the updated tooling-default claim that already includes swift-format alignment. Consolidating avoids parallel claims for the same premise.
judgements.revisedStatement: Treat swift-format’s default space indentation as evidence under the main tooling-default claim rather than as a separate standalone claim.

judgements.rationale: This is the most actionable policy statement: spaces for leading indentation, automated enforcement, and CI-backed consistency.
This is effectively the tail clause of the earlier hybrid policy claim and should be merged to keep one canonical enforcement policy.
judgements.revisedStatement: Enforcement should validate leading indentation style and avoid blanket tab bans in non-indentation contexts, as part of the main hybrid enforcement policy.

outputs.summary: Use spaces for Swift. Keep claims focused on ecosystem/tooling consistency, correct the width-specific claim (4-space default is more accurate than "2-space convention"), and merge duplicate enforcement/tooling claims into earlier canonical ones.

outputs.fullResponse:
Final vote round. The catalog is clean: eight active claims after merges, spanning four pro-spaces arguments, two bounded pro-tab observations, one tradeoff on indentation width, and one actionable enforcement recommendation. Both agents have converged without substantive disagreement. My votes are as follows.

claude-agent:0:0 (Swift community standard is spaces): Accept. This is the foundational claim of the entire debate. The Swift open-source project, Apple standard library, and both major style guides are unambiguous on spaces. Highest confidence claim in the catalog.

claude-agent:0:1 (Xcode defaults to spaces): Accept. Factually verified across Xcode versions. The 4-space default is consistent with swift-format (now merged as supporting evidence into this claim). Tooling default is among the most practical decision factors for a team.

claude-agent:0:2 (Spaces render consistently): Accept. This is a fundamental property of the characters themselves: spaces are fixed-width by definition, tabs are not. The claim is structurally sound and covers the rendering inconsistency risk that was absorbed from merged claims.

claude-agent:0:3 (Tabs enable per-developer display customization): Accept. This is the strongest pro-tab argument and the catalog is more credible for including it honestly. The caveat about formatter enforcement being a counterweight is appropriate and well-integrated. Rejecting this would make the final catalog look one-sided.

claude-agent:0:4 (Tabs are semantically intended for indentation): Accept. A low-weight but intellectually honest acknowledgment of tabs' historical purpose. The claim has been correctly downgraded in significance. Including it demonstrates the debate considered tab arguments fairly before concluding for spaces.

claude-agent:0:5 (SwiftLint and SwiftFormat enforce spaces by default): Accept. Strong and specific. Names the two dominant enforcement tools in the Swift ecosystem and correctly characterizes their default behavior. The formatter-churn consequence of switching to tabs is a real practical cost.

claude-agent:0:7 (Indentation width is a team convention): Accept. The revised statement is accurate: 4 spaces is the dominant default in Xcode-based workflows, 2 spaces appears in some guides (notably Google's), and teams should standardize on one width via tooling rather than debate it. This is a useful tradeoff framing that prevents the catalog from implying a false universal answer to the width sub-question.

codex-agent:0:5 (Enforce spaces via tooling in CI): Accept. The most actionable claim in the catalog. The enforcement-scope refinement (validate indentation, avoid blanket tab bans on string literals/generated content) was merged into this claim from codex-agent:2:0. A concrete policy recommendation that makes the debate's conclusion operational.

judgements.rationale: Foundational claim, well-evidenced by Swift.org, Apple standard library, and major style guides. No credible counterevidence emerged in four rounds.
Factually accurate. Xcode's 4-space default and swift-format alignment make this a strong tooling-default argument.
Structurally sound. Spaces are fixed-width by definition; tab rendering width is editor-configurable. The inconsistency across GitHub, terminals, and editors is verifiable.
Legitimately acknowledges the strongest pro-tab argument while correctly bounding it with the formatter-enforcement caveat. Well-balanced claim.
Low decision weight but intellectually honest. The historical semantic argument for tabs is real; the claim correctly notes it does not override ecosystem factors in Swift.
Specific and verifiable. Both SwiftLint and SwiftFormat default to spaces. The formatter-churn cost of switching to tabs is a concrete practical consequence.
Correctly revised to reflect that 4 spaces dominates in Xcode-based workflows while acknowledging 2-space guides exist. Scopes the width question as a separate team-level decision.
Actionable enforcement policy. Correctly scopes CI checks to leading indentation rather than all tab bytes, avoiding false positives in string literals and generated content.

outputs.summary: All eight active claims are accepted. The four pro-spaces claims form a coherent, mutually reinforcing argument from community standard to tooling default to rendering consistency to CI enforcement. The two pro-tab claims are accepted as honest acknowledgments of tab benefits, correctly bounded by formatter-enforcement caveats. The tradeoff claim on width is accepted as a nuanced clarification that prevents the catalog from overclaiming. The enforcement policy claim is accepted as the practical conclusion. Verdict: use spaces (default 4, or team-chosen width) in Swift, enforced via SwiftLint/SwiftFormat/.editorconfig in CI.

claimVotes.reason: The strongest single claim in the catalog. Community standard, official style guides, and Apple's own source code all point to spaces. Uncontested across all rounds.
Tooling defaults carry high practical weight. Xcode's 4-space default and swift-format alignment mean choosing tabs requires active overrides in the most common Swift development environment.
Rendering consistency is a verifiable, objective property. This claim directly explains why tabs create real friction in code review and documentation contexts.
The strongest honest argument for tabs. Accept it to keep the catalog intellectually credible. The formatter-enforcement caveat is correctly integrated.
Historically valid, appropriately downweighted. Including it demonstrates the debate examined tab arguments in good faith before concluding for spaces.
Specific, named-tool evidence with concrete consequence (formatter churn). Among the most practically useful claims for a team making this decision.
Important nuance: the spaces-vs-tabs verdict does not resolve the 2-vs-4 width question. This claim correctly separates the two decisions and prevents overclaiming.
Turns the debate conclusion into an operational policy. The enforcement-scoping detail (indentation only, not blanket tab ban) is practically important for avoiding linter false positives.

outputs.fullResponse: Final vote: choose spaces for Swift source indentation. Ecosystem convention, editor defaults, formatter/linter workflows, and cross-view consistency all favor spaces for shared codebases. Tabs still offer per-developer display flexibility, but that advantage is mostly local and should not drive repository-wide style in Swift teams. Enforcement should focus on leading indentation style in source code rather than banning every tab byte in all contexts. Indentation width is a separate policy choice; pick one width and enforce it consistently.

The core tooling-friction argument is useful, but the statement conflates tool naming/ownership and is too absolute on defaults.

Scoping enforcement to indentation avoids false positives while preserving a strict and practical style policy.

outputs.summary: Use spaces for Swift indentation, keep tab-related claims only as secondary context, and enforce indentation style via tooling with scope limited to leading indentation.
